content.body: A **digital image** is a rectangular grid of pixels. Each pixel stores a color or intensity value. This grid is called a **bitmap** — the 2D array of pixel values that represents the image.
The two-dimensional array (grid) of pixel values that represents graphics or image data.
Hardware (on the video/graphics card) that stores the bitmap so it can be displayed on screen.
The number of pixels in a digital image. More pixels = higher resolution = better quality. Example: $1600 \times 1200$ is high resolution, $640 \times 480$ is lower.

**1-bit images:** Each pixel is stored as a single bit — 0 (white/background) or 1 (black/foreground). Also called a **binary image** or **1-bit monochrome image**. Think of a black-and-white photocopy — either ink or paper. No gray, no color.

**8-bit grayscale images:** Each pixel is 1 byte (8 bits), giving values 0–255. 0 = black, 255 = white. 256 shades of gray — enough for the human eye to see smooth gradations.

content.title: Storage Calculation
content.body:
A $640 \times 480$ grayscale image: $640 \times 480 \times 1 = 307{,}200$ bytes = **300 KB**

A $640 \times 480$ 24-bit color image: $640 \times 480 \times 3 = 921{,}600$ bytes = **900 KB** (3× more)

An 8-bit image can be thought of as **8 separate 1-bit layers** stacked together.

- **Plane 7 (MSB):** Most important brightness info. If pixel value > 127, this bit is 1. Looks like a rough version of the original image.
- **Plane 0 (LSB):** Finest detail. Looks almost like random noise — represents tiny brightness differences.

content.title: Bit-Plane Breakdown — Pixel Value 200
content.body:
200 in binary = **11001000**

| Plane | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bit   | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- Plane 7 = 1 (MSB — carries most visual info)
- Plane 6 = 1
- Plane 5 = 0
- Plane 4 = 0
- Plane 3 = 1
- Plane 2, 1, 0 = 0 (LSB — contributes least)
Plane 7 alone looks like a rough grayscale version of the whole image. Plane 0 alone looks like random noise. Strip the MSB and the image becomes unrecognizable. Strip the LSB and you barely notice.

flashcards.a: Each pixel is stored as a single bit (0 or 1). 0 = white (background), 1 = black (foreground). Also called binary image or 1-bit monochrome image. Think of a black-and-white photocopy.
The two-dimensional array (grid) of pixel values that represents the graphics/image data.
Hardware on the video/graphics card that stores the bitmap so it can be displayed on screen.
The number of pixels in a digital image. More pixels = higher resolution = better quality. Example: 1600×1200 is high resolution, 640×480 is lower.

flashcards.q: How many shades can an 8-bit grayscale image represent?
flashcards.a: 256 shades of gray (values 0 to 255). 0 = black, 255 = white. 1 byte per pixel.
An 8-bit image decomposed into 8 separate 1-bit layers. Plane 7 (MSB) has the most visual info — looks like a rough version of the image. Plane 0 (LSB) looks like noise.

flashcards.q: What does pixel value 200 look like in binary? Which planes are 1?

problems.q: Calculate the pixel data storage (in bytes) of a $640 \times 480$ 8-bit grayscale image.
problems.a: Each pixel = 1 byte. Storage = $640 \times 480 \times 1 = \mathbf{307{,}200}$ bytes = 300 KB.

problems.q: A pixel has value 150. Write it in binary (8 bits) and state which bit-planes are 1.
problems.a:
$150 = 128 + 16 + 4 + 2 = 10010110$

Planes that are 1: **7, 4, 2, 1** (MSB through position 1).
Planes that are 0: **6, 5, 3, 0**.
problems.hint: Convert to binary: 150 = 128+16+4+2. Bit 7 is the leftmost (128s place).

problems.q: How much storage does a $1024 \times 768$ 32-bit image use (pixel data only)?
problems.a: $1024 \times 768 \times 4 = \mathbf{3{,}145{,}728}$ bytes (about 3 MB). Each pixel = 4 bytes (R, G, B, A).
